coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped green fluorescence carbon dots as a label-free probe for determination of cytochrome c in serum and temperature sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiantao
Chu, Huiyuan
Shen, Jiwei
Wang, Chaozhan
Wei, Yinmao
coredata.dc:description: As an important biomarker, the analysis of cytochrome c (Cyt c) plays a crucial role in cell-apoptosis or even cancer diagnosis. This work develops a label-free probe for Cyt c using the nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots (N, F-CDs) which were facile prepared through solvothermal method with 3, 4-difluorophenylhydrazine as precursor. The N, F-CDs have an average diameter of 3.4 nm, and can form a quite stable colloidal solution. The N, F-CDs show bright yellow-green fluorescence, excitation/emission wavelengths 475/530 nm, and a relatively high fluorescence quantum yield of 16.9%. Interestingly, the N, F-CDs indicate a linear and reversible variation of emission intensity with a sensitivity of −1.11% per ℃ in the temperature range from 25 to 60 ℃. Inner filter effect (IFE) between N, F-CDs and Cyt c turns the fluorescence of N, F-CDs from “on” to “off”. The sensor possesses the excellent anti-interference ability towards the main components of plasma. Under optimum conditions, there is a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity function (F0-F) and the concentration of Cyt c in the range of 0.5–25 μΜ with a limit of detection (LOD) (S/N = 3) of 0.25 μM. Finally, the developed method has been successfully used to detect Cyt c in human serum sample with satisfactory recoveries in a range of 93.14–110.40%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
Cytochrome c
Fluorescence sensor
Temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
Detection of Cyt c in human serum sample
Characterization of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
Optical properties of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
Stability of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence response of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots towards Cyt c
Mechanism of the fluorescence response of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots toward Cyt c
Optical response of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots to temperature

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of N, F-CDs. (B) Full survey of XPS spectra of N, F-CDs.
(A) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of N, F-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of N, F-CDs (4 μg mL−1) under different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N, F-CDs at various concentrations of Cyt c within a range of 0–60 μM. (B) The linear calibration plot between F0-F and the concentration of Cyt c within the range of 0.5–25 μM.
Fluorescence quenching response of N, F-CDs by Cyt c (10 μM) and interfering substances (100 μM). F and F0 are fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs in the presence and absence of Cyt c, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cyt c (black line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and emission spectrum (red line) of N, F-CDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of N, F-CDs, Cyt c, and the mixture of the N, F-CDs and Cyt c. (C) Time-resolved decay of N, F-CDs before and after adding 10 and 30 μM Cyt c. (D) The effect of temperature on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (C Cyt c = 10 μM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra for various temperatures. (B) Plot of the intensity at 530 nm versus temperature. (C) The reversible temperature-dependence of the fluorescence intensity of the N, F-CDs solution between 25 and 60 ℃.
Illustration of synthesis process of N, F-CDs and detection principle for Cyt c and temperature.
Comparison of the proposed method with the reported methods for the detection of Cyt c.
Recoveries of Cyt c in serum sample using the proposed sensor.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped green fluorescence carbon dots as a label-free probe for determination of cytochrome c in serum and temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule of the Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important biomarker, the analysis of cytochrome c (Cyt c) plays a crucial role in cell-apoptosis or even cancer diagnosis. This work develops a label-free probe for Cyt c using the nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots (N, F-CDs) which were facile prepared through solvothermal method with 3, 4-difluorophenylhydrazine as precursor. The N, F-CDs have an average diameter of 3.4 nm, and can form a quite stable colloidal solution. The N, F-CDs show bright yellow-green fluorescence, excitation/emission wavelengths 475/530 nm, and a relatively high fluorescence quantum yield of 16.9%. Interestingly, the N, F-CDs indicate a linear and reversible variation of emission intensity with a sensitivity of −1.11% per ℃ in the temperature range from 25 to 60 ℃. Inner filter effect (IFE) between N, F-CDs and Cyt c turns the fluorescence of N, F-CDs from “on” to “off”. The sensor possesses the excellent anti-interference ability towards the main components of plasma. Under optimum conditions, there is a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity function (F0-F) and the concentration of Cyt c in the range of 0.5–25 μΜ with a limit of detection (LOD) (S/N = 3) of 0.25 μM. Finally, the developed method has been successfully used to detect Cyt c in human serum sample with satisfactory recoveries in a range of 93.14–110.40%.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Cytochrome c(Cyt c), a water-soluble heme-containing protein, plays an important role in mitochondrial electron transport, apoptosis, acute liver failure, myocardial infarction, cancer treatment and traumatic injury [1] and the level of Cyt c in serum can act as an important indicator for many diseases. For instance, the concentration of Cyt c in cytosol and serum significantly increases in chemotherapy-treated patients [2] and in the patients who show systemic inflammatory response syndrome and multi organ dysfunction syndrome [3]. As such, Cyt c can serve as a preclinical biomarker for various pathologies, medical diagnosis and treatment and the detection of Cyt c is of importance in clinical diagnosis.

The traditional methods for the detection of Cyt c include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [4], high performance liquid chromatography [5], flow ctyotmery [6], western blot analysis [7] and electrochemistry [8]. In contrast, the newly developed fluorescence sensors possess great simplicity, remarkable selectivity, significant sensitivity, high accuracy, high economic effectiveness and fast analysis, so it has attracted considerable attention in detection of analytes [9]. As a new type of fluorescent material, carbon quantum dots are gradually applied to the field of analytical chemistry due to their high photostability, high biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and good water solubility [10–14]. For the determination of Cyt c, nitrogen/chloride doped carbon dots [15], carbon dots/SiO2/molecularly imprinted polymer [16,17] and carbon dots@nucleic acid aptamer [18] have been developed. However, the developed carbon dots require either complex preparation process [15–17] or tedious modification [18]. In addition, the emission wavelengths of these carbon dots are in the blue light region, so their applications in the biology-relevant fields often encounter the interference of the commonly blue auto-fluorescence in biological matrix and photodamage of ultraviolet excitation light to biological tissues [19].

This work presents a novel N, F-CDs for detecting Cyt c, which were facile prepared through solvothermal process with 3, 4-difluorophenylhydrazine as single precursor (Scheme 1 ). The N, F-CDs show green fluorescence with emission wavelength at 530 nm, being longer than the reported carbon dots, and can be directly acted as fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cyt c without further chemical modification or surface functionalization. It was also found that the fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs decreases linearly with the increasing of temperature, implying that N, F-CDs can also be used as a temperature sensor.

3, 4-difluorophenylhydrazine hydrochloride (98%) and Cyt c (95%, from pig heart) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) (98%), hemoglobin (Hb) (98%), trypsin (≥2500 u/mg) and lysozyme (≥2000 u/mg) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aspartic acid (≥99%), alanine (≥98.5%), leucine (≥98.5%), valine (≥98.5%), tryptophan (≥98.0%), phenylalanine (≥99.0%), l-cysteine (≥98.5%), glycine (≥99.5%), tyrosine (≥98.5%), glutamate (≥98.5%), threonine (≥98.5%), arginine (≥98.0%), methionine (≥99.0%), histidine (≥99.0%) and serine (98.0%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise stated, all other reagents in this work were of analytical reagent grade without further purification. The ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q Plus water purification system (≥18.25 MΩ·cm, Bedford, MA, USA).

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired using a Talos F200X transmission electron microscope (FEI, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on a TENSOR27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on PHI5000 VersaProbe III (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were obtained from AFM instrument with MultiMode 8 (Bruker Nano Inc, America) using a tapping mode. The fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). Absorption spectra were recorded on a spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence decay curves were acquired at λ ex = 374 nm on a photon-counting spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, England).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N, F-CDs were prepared through solvothermal method using 3, 4-difluorophenylhydrazine as the precursor. Briefly, 0.3 g of 3, 4-difluorophenylhydrazine hydrochloride was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol; the mixture was transferred into a 15 mL Telfon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the crude product was purified by silica gel column chromatography with dichloromethane and methanol as the eluent, collecting the yellow-green fraction. Finally, the black solid was obtained after concentrating the fraction.

Cyt c stock solution (100 μM) was prepared with water. Various volumes of Cyt c solution were mixed with 80 µL of N, F-CDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1), then phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) was added into the mixture to give rise to the final concentration of N, F-CDs of 4 μg mL−1. The fluorescence intensity of mixture solution at 530 nm was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 475 nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission were 3 and 5 nm, respectively. As controls, BSA, Hb, lysozyme, trypsin, amino acids (phenylalanine, glycine, glutamic acid, methionine, arginine, leucine, tryptophan, serine, threonine, aspartate, valine, proline) and metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+) were used to study the selectivity of the N, F-CDs towards Cyt c. All experiments were performed in three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of N, F-CDs was determined using the following equation:
Q c
=
Q s
×
I c
×
A s
×
η
c
2
I S
×
A c
×
η
s
2
where Q is the quantum yield, I refers to the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorption intensity at excitation wavelength, η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” and “c” refer to the standard and the unknown. Rhodamine B in ethanol was chosen as standard, which has the QY of 0.97 [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cyt c in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: The blood was obtained from a healthy adult volunteer at the School Hospital of Northwest University under the regulations of the Ethical Board Criteria by the Medical Ethics Committee. The blood was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 7 min to obtain the serum. The serum sample was diluted with 10 folds phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) as blank solution. Low (0.75 μM), medium (3 μM), high (10 μM) concentrations of Cyt c spiked in the blank solution were determined to obtain the recovery. The concentration of Cyt c is the average of three experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The HRTEM was adopted to observe the morphology of N, F-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1A, the N, F-CDs present small dots with a d-spacing of 0.25 nm, which belongs to the typical crystal lattice of graphitic carbon [21]. The average size of N, F-CDs is around 3.4 nm in the range of 2.2–4.8 nm (Fig. S1B), being in the range of colloidal particles (1–100 nm). The size of N, F-CDs is also evidenced from the AFM image and height profile (2.0–4.7 nm) (Fig. S1C and D). The surface structures of N, F-CDs were analyzed through FT-IR. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the peak at 3442 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of –NH2 and –OH [22]. CH stretching vibration is identified from the absorption peaks at 2957 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 [23]. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1517 cm−1 are assigned to CN/CO and CN, respectively [24]. The peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 1229 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-NH-C and C-NH [25]. The two strong peaks at 1124 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-F and CO, respectively [26,27]. Furthermore, the elemental composition of N, F-CDs was analyzed by the XPS (Fig. 1B). The full XPS spectra display the peaks at 284.00, 398.00, 530.00 and 686.00 eV, suggesting that the N, F-CDs are composed of C (82.07%), N (5.51%), O (8.46%) and F (3.96%) elements. The high resolution XPS spectra of N, F-CDs were supplemented in Supporting Material. The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s show four peaks at 283.6, 285.3, 286.5 and 287.8 eV (Fig. S2A), which are assigned to CC/CC, CN/CO, CO and C-F related carbon peaks, respectively [26,28]. The N 1 s band contains four peaks at 397.6 eV for graphite-like N, 398.4 eV for pyridinic-like N, 399.3 eV for pyrrolic-like N and 400.2 eV for NH groups [29], respectively (Fig. S2B). The O 1 s band exhibits two peaks at 530.5 and 531.7 eV (Fig. S2C), which are related to CO and COH/COC [30], respectively. Moreover, the F 1 s band contains one main band at 686.0 eV, which is ascribed to C-F [26] (Fig. S2D). These results illustrate that N and F are doped in the N, F-CDs and the surfaces of N, F-CDs have rich polar groups. The polar groups improve the hydrophilicity and colloidal stability of the N, F-CDs in aqueous system, and provide the possibility for the interaction between the N, F-CDs and Cyt c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the optical properties of the N, F-CDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the specific absorption peak at 475 nm suggests large amounts of sp2-bonded carbon in N, F-CDs [31]. In Fig. 2B, the N, F-CDs exhibit the same green fluorescence emission at 530 nm under different excitation wavelengths, which shows excitation-independent property. The optical behavior can be ascribed to the uniform size and surface state of sp2 clusters in N, F-CDs [32]. The QY of the N, F-CDs in ethanol is up to 16.9% (excitation wavelength, 495 nm). At present, there are two hypotheses of bandgap transitions of conjugated π-domains and surface defect-derived origins for the fluorescence mechanism of carbon quantum dots [33]. In type of bandgap transitions of conjugated π-domains, there often appears the strong absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region but the weak or no fluorescence emission, which arouses from electronic transitions. However, the N, F-CDs have strong UV absorption and strong fluorescence emission, so this type cannot be the fluorescence mechanism of N, F-CDs. Instead, surface related defective sites are reasonable to explain the fluorescence mechanism of N, F-CDs. In carbon quantum dots, there are sp2 and sp3 hybridised carbons and other functionalized surface defects [34], such as carbonyl-related localized electronic states [35]. These non-perfect sp2 domains can result in surface energy traps and give rise to the multicolour emissions that are concentrated in the blue and green regions of the visible light spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, the colloidal stability of carbon quantum dots is very important. As shown in Fig. S3, there is no coagulation and no shift of emission wavelength for 12 days for N, F-CDs solution. This reflects a long-term colloidal stability of the N, F-CDs. The measured zeta potential of N, F-CDs is −12.4 mV at pH 7.0 (Fig. S4), at which the carboxyl in N, F-CDs are deprotonated. Thus, good colloidal stability is ascribed to electrostatic repulsion among N, F-CDs [36]. In addition, ionic strength has little effect on colloidal stability (Fig. S5A).

The photostability of N, F-CDs were investigated through light exposure. As shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs remains unchanged even after continuous irradiation with a UV light lamp of 365 nm for 60 min. The good photostability and colloidal stability suggest the potential of N, F-CDs in practical application.

Fig. S6A shows that the fluorescence intensity of the N, F-CDs is quenched with the addition of Cyt c, which suggests that the N, F-CDs can be used as a fluorescence probe of Cyt c. To achieve effectively the detection of Cyt c, the influence of time on the quenching efficiency was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the N, F-CDs is quenched within 2 min by Cyt c and remains unchanged for 20 min (Fig. S6B), which indicates that the N, F-CDs can detect Cyt c in a fast time. Hence, the response time was chosen 2 min for further experiments.

In general, pH of solution has significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of most of the reported carbon quantum dots [37]. However, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared N, F-CDs has no remarkable change in the range of pH 2–11 (Fig. S6C). This phenomenon can be understood in terms of the charge change in the N, F-CDs due to protonation and de-protonation of amino and carboxylic on the surface of N, F-CDs [38]. In addition, the quenching efficiency is also little affected by pH over the wide range from 5 to 11 (Fig. S6D). Taking into account the neutral physiological condition, pH 7.0 was chosen for the subsequent study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots towards Cyt c
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensities of the mixture of N, F-CDs and various concentrations of Cyt c were recorded (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescence intensities of the mixtures gradually decrease with increasing Cyt c concentration from 0 to 60 μM, while the peak patterns of the fluorescence spectra almost keep the same (Fig. 3A). There is a good linear relationship between the net fluorescence decrease (F0-F) and the Cyt c concentration in the range of 0.5–25 μM (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 3B). Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N, F-CDs before and after the treatment with Cyt c, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of Cyt c was calculated to be 0.25 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Such a low LOD and a linear range can fully meet the determination requirement of Cyt c in the serum of patients with myocardial infarction, in which Cyt c is usually in the concentration range of 0.81 ~ 20.16 μM [39].

To indicate the advantages of the N, F-CDs probe, the comparison of the N, F-CDs probe with the reported fluorescence probes based carbon dots is conducted in terms of LOD, linear range, emission wavelength and QY (Table 1 ). It is found that the N, F-CDs probe presents comparable linear ranges, LOD and QY but has the longest emission wavelength among the reported CDs. The probe with longer emission wavelength can offer a deeper light penetration into biological tissues and show less photodamage in practical application [42].

Actual samples are often complex and many co-existing substances may interfere the determination of target analyte. To evaluate the anti-interference of the probe, a number of substances are added as interference into the detect system. The selected substances are the main components in blood, including BSA, Hb, lysozyme, trypsin, amino acids (phenylalanine, glycine, glutamic acid, methionine, arginine, leucine, tryptophan, serine, threonine, aspartate, valine, proline) and some metal ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cu2+). When the concentration of the added interference is 10-fold Cyt c (N, F-CDs/Cyt c as blank), all the fluorescence intensities of the mixture are found almost unchanged (less than 5.8% fluctuation) (Fig. 4 ). The result indicated that the N, F-CDs probe has good anti-interference property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the fluorescence response of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots toward Cyt c
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of N, F-CDs by Cyt c was investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, the characteristic adsorptions of Cyt c are located at 407 nm, 526 nm and 548 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence emission wavelength of the N, F- CDs is 530 nm. As a result, the absorption spectrum of Cyt c coincides with the emission band of N, F-CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) may occur between the N, F- CDs and Cyt c. To ascertain which one is the real quenching mechanism, the FT-IR spectra of N, F-CDs and/or Cyt c were recorded and fluorescence lifetimes was determined. In FT-IR spectra (Fig. 5B), the absorption peak of –NH2 at 3442 cm−1 in N, F-CDs shifts to 3418 cm−1 while the absorption peaks of CN (1229 cm−1) and CO (1063 cm−1) are weakened after the addition of Cyt c, implying the formation of N, F-CDs/Cyt c complex. Electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bonding are responsible for the formation of the complex between Cyt c and N, F-CDs. The presence of electrostatic attraction is obvious because isoelectric point of Cyt c is about 9.8 [43] and the zeta potential of N, F-CDs is −12.4 mV (pH 7.0). The hydrogen bonding can be formed between hydrogen bond donor and acceptor on the surface of N, F-CDs and Cyt c, i.e., carbonyl, amino and hydroxyl groups. For the fluorescence lifetimes of the N, F-CDs (Fig. 5C and Table S1), there are no significant changes before and after adding different concentrations of Cyt c. These results suggest that the IFE rather than FRET is the quenching mechanism of N, F-CDs by Cyt c [44]. In order to further support the conclusion, the effect of temperature on the sensing of Cyt c was investigated (Fig. 5D). The temperature has little effect on fluorescence quenching efficiency, which is another indicator of the presence of IFE [45]. In a word, these results confirm that the fluorescence quenching is indeed the IFE process. This mechanism also explains the excellent anti-interference of the probe. Because there is no overlap between the absorption peaks of interfering substances and the emission peak of N, F-CDs except for Cyt c (Fig. S7), the probe has good anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response of nitrogen and fluorine co-doped carbon dots to temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs gradually decreases with the increase of temperature. This phenomenon is due to the thermal activation of nonradiative-decay pathways [46]. Once the temperature is increased, the non-radiative channel is activated so that more excited electrons can return to the ground state through the non-radiative transition, decreasing the fluorescence intensity. Also, it is found that there is a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm and temperature (R2 = 0.989) and the slop of the line is −1.11% per ℃(Fig. 6B). Moreover, the maximum fluorescence intensity could still be retained 84.7% during consecutive four heating and cooling cycles over the temperature range of 25–60 ℃ (Fig. 6C), indicating the fluorescence of N, F-CDs is reversible and recoverable. The good linear relationship and the stable temperature sensing can meet the requirement on the temperature sensor [47]. Most importantly, the slope of the line is higher than those of the reported temperature sensors based on carbon dots (-0.62 - −0.84% per ℃) [48–53]. Thus, the N, F-CDs can be utilized as a temperature sensor with higher temperature-sensitivity.

In the serum of healthy people, Cyt c is normally at the nanomolar level [54]. However, the content of Cyt c can significantly increase for some diseases, e.g. the levels in the serum of patients with myocardial infarction can reach up to 0.81 ~ 20.16 μM [39]. To explore the practicability of N, F-CDs probe in real sample, the diluted serum of healthy people acts as a blank because the concentration of Cyt c is so low (nanomolar level) that it can be ignored relative to the level of Cyt c in patient serum. Therefore, the diluted serum of healthy people was inoculated with different concentrations of Cyt c and then the content of Cyt c was detected by the N, F-CDs sensor. As displayed in Table 2 , the recoveries of Cyt c in the spiked samples are in the range of 93.14–111.40% with RSD less than 8.57% (n = 3). Particularly, the serum as low as 0.75 μM Cyt c is measured well, which covers the concentration of Cyt c in patients with myocardial infarction [39]. The result indicates that the fluorescent sensor based N, F-CDs can be applied in the determination of Cyt C in serum sample.

Finally, the cytotoxicity of N, F-CDs was evaluated according to 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) method [55]. As shown in Fig. S8, after hela cells were cultured with 50 μg mL-1N, F-CDs for 12 h, the cell viability remains up to 80%. The result indicates little cytotoxicity of N, F-CDs to cell. Moreover, N, F-CDs probe can offer longer emission wavelength, causing less photodamage towards biological tissues. Therefore, these properties allow N, F-CDs probe for the potential use in biomedical applications.

A novel N, F-CDs was synthesized for the detection of Cyt c and temperature sensor through a one-step solvothermal method. The N, F-CDs forms a stable colloidal solution and the surface of N, F-CDs has abundant polar groups which can interact with Cyt c. The use of fluoride phenylhydrazine as precursor endows the N, F-CDs with special optical and temperature response properties. Compared with the reported carbon dots for sensing Cyt c [15,17,18,40,31], the probe in this work has longer excitation and emission wavelength, suggesting a deeper light penetration into biological tissues and less photodamage in biomedical application. The fluorescence quenching of N, F-CDs by Cyt c is attributed to the IFE mechanism. In analysis of Cyt c, the probe has good anti-interference and has a low LOD that covers the concentration range of Cyt c in patients with myocardial infarction. As for temperature response, the fluorescence intensity of N, F-CDs is more sensitive to temperature since plot of the fluorescence intensity against temperature yields a slope of 1.11% per ℃, being higher than those of the reported temperature sensors based on carbon dots (0.62–0.84% per ℃) [47–53]. Therefore, the N, F-CDs can act as a promising candidate for the detection of Cyt c as well as a temperature sensor with higher sensitivity.

Jiantao Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing-original draft, Investigation. Huiyuan Chu: Software, Validation. Jiwei Shen: Writing - review & editing. Chaozhan Wang: Resources. Yinmao Wei: Conceptualization, Writing review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775121 and 21974106).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.10.138.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in cytochrome c biosensing technologies
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Manickam, A. Kaushik, C. Karunakaran, S. Bhansali, Recent advances in cytochrome c biosensing technologies, Biosens. Bioelectron. 87 (2017) 654-668.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cytochrome c is a possible new marker for fulminant hepatitis in humans
